skill.name: microsoft-foundry
skill.description: Work with Microsoft Azure AI Foundry projects, resources, agents, model deployments, quotas, RBAC, and troubleshooting workflows.
skill.summary: Manage Azure AI Foundry resources, agents, deployments, quota, and access workflows.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and environment patterns, but review found they are mainly Markdown workflow examples and Azure administration instructions, not hidden executable code. The skill is not malicious, but it can guide cloud resource creation, RBAC changes, service principal setup, Docker builds, and secret handling, so it should publish with an operational risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Azure Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill provides workflows that run Azure CLI commands to create projects, create AI Services resources, deploy containers, manage quotas, and assign RBAC roles. This is expected behavior for the skill, but misuse can change cloud infrastructure, create costs, or grant access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain direct Azure CLI workflows for provisioning and role management. The context is legitimate administration, not covert execution.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Configuration Handling
medium_findings.description: The deployment workflow scans and carries environment variables into agent payloads, generates local .env files, and documents commands that return access keys or service principal passwords. The skill includes masking and least privilege guidance, but users must avoid exposing secrets in chat logs or generated files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence clearly shows environment-variable collection and commands that can output credentials. The files also present these as normal setup steps, which reduces malicious-intent confidence.

medium_findings.title: Non-Interactive Cloud Changes
medium_findings.description: Several workflows support non-interactive execution or auto-accept options, including azd provisioning, Azure resource creation with --yes, and hosted-agent deployment defaults. This can be useful for automation, but it increases the chance of unintended resource creation or configuration changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static findings are documentation, but the semantic behavior is real if a user asks the assistant to execute the workflow. Human confirmation and Azure permission boundaries remain important controls.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts are Markdown inline code, fenced code blocks, or command examples. They are not Ruby backtick execution in a source file, although they document commands an assistant may run with user approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contents are Markdown documentation and examples. There is no executable Ruby source or backtick operator context in the reviewed lines.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Prompt Role Token Injection
low_findings.description: The reported role-token alert corresponds to Python sample code that checks message roles and emits assistant output. I found no instruction to override evaluator rules, skip analysis, or impersonate a higher-priority system message.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious text is normal SDK sample code using Role.ASSISTANT and agent instructions. A targeted injection keyword search found no malicious override language in the skill files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Path Traversal Pattern in Relative Links
low_findings.description: The filesystem alerts are caused by relative Markdown links such as references to sibling skills. These are navigation paths in documentation, not user-controlled file reads or writes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain documentation links and workflow references. No evidence found of path traversal being applied to filesystem APIs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure CLI Resource Mutation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes command examples for provisioning resource groups, Azure AI Services accounts, deployments, and RBAC assignments. These are legitimate for the skill purpose but require user authorization and Azure permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and operational. They are not hidden, obfuscated, or unrelated to the marketplace description.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential and Key Output Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents access-key retrieval and service-principal creation. This can expose secrets if copied into logs or chats without redaction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands can return or consume secret material. The context is administrative setup, so the issue is operational risk rather than confirmed abuse.

dangerous_patterns.title: Container Build and Push Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The deploy workflow builds and pushes container images to Azure Container Registry, then updates hosted agent definitions. This is expected but can publish unreviewed project code if run without careful review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Docker and ACR workflow is clearly documented. The risk depends on user intent and project contents, not on hidden malicious behavior in the skill.

content.value_statement: Azure AI Foundry work often spans agents, models, quotas, access, and deployment steps. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured workflows for those operations.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of Azure AI Foundry projects and AI Services resources.
Provides workflows for creating, deploying, invoking, and troubleshooting Foundry agents.
Documents Docker and Azure Container Registry deployment paths for hosted agents.
Helps inspect quotas, choose regions, and plan model deployment capacity.
Explains RBAC role assignment, managed identity access, and service principal setup.
Includes Python SDK examples for agents, RAG, evaluation, and cleanup patterns.
limitations: Requires an Azure subscription, Azure CLI access, and suitable Foundry permissions.
Can create billable Azure resources when workflows are executed.
Does not replace Azure policy, cost management, or security review.
Some examples depend on preview SDKs or changing Azure service behavior.

use_cases.title: Set Up Foundry Infrastructure
use_cases.description: Create a project, resource group, AI Services account, and related environment values for a new Foundry workspace.

use_cases.title: Deploy and Test AI Agents
use_cases.description: Package hosted agents, push images to Azure Container Registry, update agent definitions, and verify invocation results.

use_cases.title: Govern Access and Capacity
use_cases.description: Audit RBAC assignments, configure identities, inspect regional quota, and plan model deployments before production rollout.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Foundry Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure AI Foundry project setup and tell me which values or permissions are missing before I deploy an agent.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner validation before running cloud workflows

prompt_templates.title: Create a Foundry Resource
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create an Azure AI Services Foundry resource in a region I choose, and show each confirmation before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy a Hosted Agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Deploy this existing agent project as a hosted Foundry agent using ACR cloud build, then invoke it with a test message.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Secure Production Rollout
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the RBAC, quota, model deployment, environment variables, and troubleshooting plan for this Foundry production release.

output_examples.input: Can you deploy my agent to Foundry?
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies project type, confirms required environment variables, proposes a Docker or cloud build path, and asks before making Azure changes.

output_examples.input: Why did my model deployment fail with quota errors?
output_examples.output: The assistant checks regional quota usage, compares required capacity with available capacity, and recommends another region or quota request.

output_examples.input: Who can access this Foundry resource?
output_examples.output: The assistant lists relevant RBAC scopes, explains assigned roles, and highlights missing or excessive permissions for review.

best_practices: Confirm subscription, resource group, region, and cost impact before running provisioning commands.
Mask secrets and review generated .env or service principal output before sharing logs.
Use least privilege roles and managed identities instead of broad owner access where possible.
anti_patterns: Do not run non-interactive provisioning against an unknown subscription or tenant.
Do not paste access keys, client secrets, or full environment files into public chats.
Do not assign Contributor or Owner roles when a narrower Foundry role is enough.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps manage Azure AI Foundry projects, resources, agents, deployments, quotas, RBAC, and troubleshooting workflows.

faq.question: Does it run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: It provides command workflows and may run them when the user asks. Users should confirm subscription, region, and cost impact first.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium operational warning. The reviewed findings are legitimate cloud administration behavior, not confirmed malware.

faq.question: What permissions are required?
faq.answer: Many workflows require Azure login and roles such as Contributor, Azure AI User, Azure AI Owner, or role assignment permissions.

faq.question: Can it expose secrets?
faq.answer: It can handle environment variables, access keys, and service principal credentials. Users should mask and store secrets securely.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
